program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: Saying It Better -- When You Don't Have the Word
lesson.menu_desc: The fluency lesson: what to do when the exact word is gone. Instead of freezing, you describe the thing until the other person understands -- a small tool, a bottle-opener, a plug adapter, all bought without knowing a single name. The toolkit: it's a kind of..., it's used for -ing..., it's made of..., it's a thing that / a place where / a person who..., it's similar to..., it's the opposite of.... New describing words: a gadget, stuff, to describe, similar to, a kind of, round, flat, made of, used for, the opposite of -- 39 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- Saying It Better
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 16 -- Saying It Better
lesson.grammar_point: describing something without its name: circumlocution frames and defining relative clauses (it's a kind of, it's used for -ing, it's made of, a thing that/which, a place where, a person who, it's similar to)

listenings.text: My name is Marco, and I have worked in a small shop in Lisbon for twenty years, so let me tell you a secret about tourists. Every day, someone comes to my counter, and they need something, and they do not know the word in English or in Portuguese. And I watch the fear arrive on their face. Some of them turn red, they say sorry, sorry, and they walk out with empty hands, and they go home without the thing they came for. And it breaks my heart, because they did not need the word. They needed twenty more seconds and a little courage. Here is what the clever ones do. They do not run. They say: it is a small thing, it is made of metal, and it is used for opening a bottle of wine. And of course, in one second, I know: a corkscrew. Or they say: it is a kind of sticker, it is used for a small cut on your finger. A plaster. Done. I do not need you to know the word. I need you to describe the thing. Tell me what it is made of. Tell me what it is used for. Tell me what it is similar to, or the opposite of. Is it round, is it flat, is it big, is it small? Point at it, draw it in the air with your hands, compare it to something I know. I have been doing this for twenty years, in four languages, and I promise you: nobody has ever left my shop because their grammar was wrong. They leave because they gave up too soon. So do not give up. The word is not the point. The thing is the point. Describe it, and it is yours.

I am Dulce, I am sixty-nine, and for most of my life, one forgotten word could close my mouth for the rest of the day. I would be in the middle of a sentence, in a shop or at a dinner, and the word would simply go, like a light switching off, and I would stop. I would look down at my hands, and I would feel small and stupid, and I would say nothing, and the moment would pass, and somebody else would speak for me. For years I believed that talking meant knowing every word. And then a young teacher told me something that changed my trips completely. She said: Dulce, you already know two thousand words. You will always be missing the exact one you want, in every language, even in Portuguese. That is normal. Even people born speaking English forget words and go around them. The trick is not to have every word. The trick is to never stop. If you lose the word for umbrella, you say: the thing you open when it rains. If you lose the word for pharmacy, you say: the place where you buy medicine. You walk around the missing word, like walking around a hole in the road, and you keep going, and the other person follows you every time. So now, when a word disappears, I do not go quiet anymore. I describe. It is a kind of, it is used for, it is made of, it is similar to. I talk my way around it, and I arrive. And I have never once, in five years, been left standing in a shop with my mouth closed, waiting for a word that was not going to come. Describe it, and keep walking. The word will catch up with you later, and it does not matter if it never does.

structure.title: Six little frames to walk around any missing word
cards: It's a kind of... / a type of...

Start with the big group, so the other person knows the area: "It's a kind of bag." "It's a type of coat." "It's a kind of small tool." This one sentence points them at the shelf before you say anything else.
It's used for + -ing / It's made of...

Say what it does and what it is built from: "It's used for opening bottles." "It's used for cleaning your glasses." "It's made of metal." "It's made of plastic." After "for", the verb always wears -ing (opening, cutting) -- the shape you learned last time.
A thing that... / a place where... / a person who...

Attach a small sentence to name the thing: "It's a thing that you use in the rain." "It's a place where you buy medicine." "It's a person who cuts your hair." Use that/which for things, where for places, who for people -- never who for a thing.
It's similar to... / It's the opposite of...

When the word is gone, borrow a word they have: "It's similar to a spoon, but smaller." "It's like a small umbrella." "It's the opposite of hot." One comparison, and the picture appears in their head.

paras: It is a rainy Tuesday in Porto, and Rosângela is standing in a small general store, the kind that sells a little of everything. She needs three things, and she knows the English word for exactly none of them. The old Rosângela — the one from four years ago in Paris — would have looked down at her hands, said "sorry, sorry," and walked out into the rain with nothing. But the young man behind the counter, Tomás, speaks a little English, and this time she does not run. She takes a breath, and instead of the word she does not have, she uses the ones she does. "Excuse me," she says. "I don't know the word. Let me describe it. It's a small gadget, it's made of plastic, and it's used for putting my Brazilian charger into your walls — the holes here are different." Tomás smiles. "Ah — an adapter," he says, and puts one on the counter.

"Perfect. And I need one more thing." She thinks for a second, and builds it, piece by piece. "It's a little tool, it's made of metal, and it's used for opening a bottle of wine. It's a kind of... spiral, that goes into the cork." "A corkscrew," says Tomás, already reaching for the shelf. "And the last one?" This one is harder, and she slows down. "It's a kind of small sticker, for your skin. It's similar to a little bandage — you put it on a small cut on your finger." "Plasters," he says, and there they are, a whole box of them. Three things she could not name, all three now sitting on the counter in front of her. She did not know one single word — adapter, corkscrew, plaster — and she got every one.

Here is what Tomás does not know. For forty years, a forgotten word was a locked door for Rosângela. She would be talking, and the word would vanish, and she would go quiet, and her son, whose English is perfect, would finish the sentence for her. She was grateful, and it was easier, and that was exactly the problem. Because the word was never really the point. Tomás did not understand her because her English was perfect — it is not. He understood her because she told him what the thing was made of, what it was used for, and what it was similar to. That is not a trick for beginners; it is what every good speaker does, in every language, all the time. You will always be missing the exact word. The skill is not to have it. The skill is to walk around it, out loud, and keep going — until the other person nods, and reaches for the shelf.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Rosângela cannot buy anything because she does not know the English words for the three things she needs, so she leaves the shop empty-handed.

Instead of giving up when she does not know a word, Rosângela describes each thing -- what it is made of, what it is used for, what it is similar to -- and gets all three, because describing matters more than knowing the exact word.
Tomás understands Rosângela only because her English is perfect and she never makes a single mistake.

items: Rosângela knew the English words for all three things she needed before she went into the shop.
The opposite: she knew the word for none of them -- not adapter, not corkscrew, not plaster. She described each one instead: "I don't know the word. Let me describe it."
To ask for the adapter, she said it was made of plastic and used for putting her charger into the walls.
She says it exactly: "It's a small gadget, it's made of plastic, and it's used for putting my Brazilian charger into your walls."
She described the corkscrew as a little tool, made of metal, used for opening a bottle of wine.
Word for word: "It's a little tool, it's made of metal, and it's used for opening a bottle of wine... a kind of spiral, that goes into the cork."
When Rosângela could not remember a word, she gave up and walked out of the shop with nothing.
The old Rosângela from Paris would have done that. This time she did not run -- she described all three things, and got every one.
Tomás understood her because her English was perfect, not because of her descriptions.
The text says her English is not perfect. He understood because she told him what each thing was made of, used for, and similar to -- the description, not the grammar.

items.situation: You are in a shop and you need a corkscrew, but the word is gone. Describe it so the shopkeeper understands.
tips: It's a small tool, made of metal, used for opening a bottle of wine.
It's a kind of little gadget you use to open wine. It goes into the cork.

items.situation: You need a plug adapter to charge your phone, and you don't know the word. Describe it.
tips: It's a small thing, made of plastic, used for putting my charger into your walls.
It's a kind of gadget for the electricity -- the holes here are different from Brazil.

items.situation: You forgot the word "umbrella". Describe it, using what it is used for.
tips: It's a thing that you open when it rains, to stay dry.
It's used for the rain -- you hold it over your head.

items.situation: You are looking for a pharmacy, but the word is gone. Describe the place.
tips: It's a place where you buy medicine.
It's a kind of shop for medicine, when you are sick.

items.situation: You need scissors. Don't say the word -- describe what they are used for and what they are made of.
tips: It's a small tool, made of metal, used for cutting paper.
It's a thing that you use to cut -- two sharp parts that open and close.

items.situation: You want to describe a backpack without the word. Say what kind of thing it is and where you wear it.
tips: It's a kind of bag that you wear on your back.
It's similar to a bag, but you carry it on your shoulders, on your back.

items.situation: The shopkeeper brings the wrong thing. Politely say it is the opposite, and describe what you actually want.
tips: Sorry, that's the opposite -- I need a small one, not a big one.
Almost! It's similar to that, but it's flat and round, not tall.

items.situation: You forget a word in the middle of a sentence. Don't go quiet -- say the sentence that buys you time to describe it.
tips: Sorry, I don't know the word -- let me describe it.
How do you say... it's a thing that you use for cleaning your glasses?
